text:
Traditional animal models and static cell cultures frequently fail to replicate the complex human intestinal microenvironment, leaving the specific mechanisms of mercury ion (Hg(II)) transport poorly understood. To bridge this gap, we developed a biomimetic gut-on-a-chip that integrates transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) and electrochemical sensors for the real-time, in situ monitoring of cellular physiology and heavy metal absorption. 

The device recreates the dynamic conditions of the living intestine by applying physiological fluid shear stress (0.02 dyne/cm²) and cyclic mechanical strain (1%, 0.15 Hz). These stimuli promoted the formation of a robust physical barrier and the spontaneous development of intestinal villi-like structures. Using the integrated sensor array, we simultaneously tracked the transport of Hg(II) and assessed the resulting cellular damage across varying concentrations. Our findings reveal that mechanical forces significantly influence mercury uptake: increasing tensile strain from 1% to 5% led to a 23.59% rise in Hg(II) absorption. This increase correlated with the upregulated expression of the mechanosensitive channel Piezo1 and the metal transporter DMT1, suggesting a mechanical-biochemical link in the intestinal handling of heavy metals. This platform provides a sophisticated tool for toxicological studies and the exploration of human-specific transport mechanisms.
